items.span_text: Our results show that: • All models benefit from tool interaction and natural language feedback, with absolute performance gains by 1–8% for each additional turn of tool use, and 2–17% with natural language feedback. • Better single-turn performance does not necessarily entail better multi-turn performance.
items.title: MINT: Evaluating LLMs in Multi-turn Interaction with Tools and Language Feedback

items.span_text: In this section, we discuss (1) how to evaluate multi-turn interaction (§2.1) with tool use and language feedback under different settings; (2) how we repurpose existing datasets for MINT evaluation (§2.2).
items.title: MINT: Evaluating LLMs in Multi-turn Interaction with Tools and Language Feedback

items.span_text: (a) LLMs generally benefit from tools and language feedback, with performance gains (absolute, same below) of 1–8% for each turn of tool use and 2–17% with natural language feedback.
items.title: MINT: Evaluating LLMs in Multi-turn Interaction with Tools and Language Feedback

items.span_text: On top of LLM-tool interaction, we use gpt-4-0613 to simulate user feedback for evaluated LLMs (Fig.
items.title: MINT: Evaluating LLMs in Multi-turn Interaction with Tools and Language Feedback

items.span_text: We compare the performance between (1) simulated language feedback and (2) lazy userLLM interaction, both in the setting of tool-augmented interaction with an interaction limit k = 5.
items.title: MINT: Evaluating LLMs in Multi-turn Interaction with Tools and Language Feedback

items.span_text: τ -bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains Shunyu Yao∗
items.title: tau-bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains

items.span_text: Existing benchmarks [27, 29, 12, 14, 16] for language agents often feature simplified instruction-following setups, where the agent autonomously interacts with an environment (web, code terminal, or APIs) given all the information upfront, without any human-inthe-loop interaction and without the need to consult any domain-specific guidelines.
items.title: tau-bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains

items.span_text: In this work, we introduce τ -bench (short for Tool-Agent-User Interaction Benchmark) to measure an agent’s ability to interact with (simulated) human users and programmatic APIs while following domain-specific policies in a consistent manner. τ -bench is built in a modular framework with (1) realistic databases and APIs, (2) domain-specific policy documents, and (3) instructions for ∗
items.title: tau-bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains

items.span_text: Most existing benchmarks for agents and task-oriented dialogue systems focus on evaluating either conversational or tool-use capabilities. τ -bench aims to unify both under realistic settings, while also testing how well agents can follow domain-specific policies in a consistent manner.
items.title: tau-bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains

items.span_text: Benchmarks for language agents and tool use.
items.title: tau-bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains

items.span_text: However, current evaluation protocols often emphasize benchmark performance with single-turn exchanges, neglecting the nuanced interactions among the user, LLMs, and external tools, while also underestimating the importance of natural language feedback from users.
items.title: MINT: Evaluating LLMs in Multi-turn Interaction with Tools and Language Feedback

items.span_text: We expect MINT can help measure progress and incentivize research in improving LLMs’ capabilities in multi-turn interactions, especially for opensource communities where multi-turn human evaluation can be less accessible compared to commercial LLMs with a larger user base.
items.title: MINT: Evaluating LLMs in Multi-turn Interaction with Tools and Language Feedback

items.span_text: However, current LLM evaluations predominantly focus on single-turn input-output (Hendrycks et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2021) and often overlook user-provided natural language feedback (Liu et al., 2023d; Deng et al., 2023b; Yang et al., 2023a; Shridhar et al., 2020), creating a discrepancy between real-world use cases and evaluation.
items.title: MINT: Evaluating LLMs in Multi-turn Interaction with Tools and Language Feedback

items.span_text: We expect that MINT can help track progress and incentivize future research in improving LLM’s multi-turn task-solving and/or feedback-providing capabilities, especially for open-source communities where human evaluation can be less accessible than commercial LLMs with a large user base.
items.title: MINT: Evaluating LLMs in Multi-turn Interaction with Tools and Language Feedback

items.span_text: For example, with the help of language feedback, even the best open-source model, Lemur-70b-chat-v1, lags behind the best closed-source model by 8.7% in absolute success rate. • On most LLMs we evaluated, models trained with supervised instruction fine-tuning (SIFT, Wei et al., 2022) and reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF, Ouyang et al., 2022a) perform worse in multi-turn settings regardless of the presence of language feedback.
items.title: MINT: Evaluating LLMs in Multi-turn Interaction with Tools and Language Feedback

items.span_text: In what follows, we introduce three settings with increased interaction complexities to measure different aspects of multi-turn interaction.
items.title: MINT: Evaluating LLMs in Multi-turn Interaction with Tools and Language Feedback

items.span_text: We consider Success Rate SR as our evaluation metric, which measures the percentage of successful task instances.
items.title: MINT: Evaluating LLMs in Multi-turn Interaction with Tools and Language Feedback

items.span_text: We also propose a new metric (pass^k) to evaluate the reliability of agent behavior over multiple trials.
items.title: tau-bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains

items.span_text: We constructed τ -bench in three stages, including manual schema and API design, LM-assisted generation of data entries, and manual scenario generation and verification for the user simulator.
items.title: tau-bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains

items.span_text: Our work uses such realistic user simulation to provide an accurate assessment of the reliability and robustness of AI agents for deployment in systems that undertake millions of real-world interactions with humans.
items.title: tau-bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains

items.span_text: Task annotation is not visible to the agent and is used only for user simulation and evaluation.
items.title: tau-bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains

items.span_text: For tasks like code generation with good verification techniques (unit tests), the community has defined the pass@k (pass at k) metric as the chance that at least one out of k i.i.d. task trials is successful, which captures the trend of agents enabling discovery of solutions with scaling of inference-time compute [5].
items.title: tau-bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains

items.span_text: For real-world agent tasks requiring reliability and consistency like customer service, we propose a new metric – pass^k (pass hat k), defined as the chance that all k i.i.d. task trials are successful, averaged across tasks.
items.title: tau-bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains

items.span_text: τ -bench defines domain-agnostic environment and user simulation classes shared by various domains, and domain-specific data in terms of database JSON files, database API Python code and documentation, domain policy text, and task instances.
items.title: tau-bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains

items.span_text: MINT mirrors the real-world user-LLM-tool collaborative problem-solving setting.
items.title: MINT: Evaluating LLMs in Multi-turn Interaction with Tools and Language Feedback

items.span_text: To solve a problem, the LLM can use external tools by generating and executing Python programs (Wang et al., 2024) and/or collecting natural language feedback to refine its solutions; the feedback is provided by GPT-4 (OpenAI, 2023), aiming to simulate human users in a reproducible and scalable way.2 For a comprehensive evaluation, we include eight established datasets spanning reasoning, code generation, and decision-making (§2.2).
items.title: MINT: Evaluating LLMs in Multi-turn Interaction with Tools and Language Feedback

items.span_text: MINT aims to simulate real-world applications of LLMs, emphasizing user-LLM and LLM-tool interactions.
items.title: MINT: Evaluating LLMs in Multi-turn Interaction with Tools and Language Feedback

items.span_text: We adopt code as a unified tool interface due to its flexibility and performance, as demonstrated by Wang et al.
items.title: MINT: Evaluating LLMs in Multi-turn Interaction with Tools and Language Feedback

items.span_text: The LLM is given two attempts to propose solutions for each problem, with a limit on the number of interaction turns k (§3.1).
items.title: MINT: Evaluating LLMs in Multi-turn Interaction with Tools and Language Feedback

items.span_text: 1), or when the k-th turn of interaction is reached.
items.title: MINT: Evaluating LLMs in Multi-turn Interaction with Tools and Language Feedback

items.span_text: We vary k ∈ {1, 2, 3, 4, 5} and compare the models’ success rate with each k.
items.title: MINT: Evaluating LLMs in Multi-turn Interaction with Tools and Language Feedback

items.span_text: We consider LLM’s performance gain w.r.t. k and the absolute performance at k = 5 as their tool-augmented task-solving ability (§3.2).
items.title: MINT: Evaluating LLMs in Multi-turn Interaction with Tools and Language Feedback

items.span_text: We also introduce the metric of pass^k, which measures the consistency and robustness of the agent across k i.i.d. trials.
items.title: tau-bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains

items.span_text: τ -bench: A benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction
items.title: tau-bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains

items.span_text: We use a language model (gpt-4-0613) to simulate a human user interacting with the agent.

items.title: tau-bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains

items.span_text: Weaker models and methods struggle with even more basic failures such as hallucinating arguments — for example, while gpt-4o FC agent only makes 0.46 tool calls with non-existent user/product/order/item IDs per τ -retail task, gpt-3.5-turbo FC / Act agents make 2.08 / 6.34, respectively.
items.title: tau-bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains

items.span_text: We execute the code and use gpt-4 to refine the code based on execution for a few initial iterations, and manually refine the code to polish minor details.
items.title: tau-bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains

items.span_text: LLMs interacting with a lazy user.
items.title: MINT: Evaluating LLMs in Multi-turn Interaction with Tools and Language Feedback

items.span_text: We consider the scenario where a user provides an initial instruction and makes minimal efforts to guide the LLM toward the final solution.
items.title: MINT: Evaluating LLMs in Multi-turn Interaction with Tools and Language Feedback

items.span_text: This setting covers real-world problems that are “hard to solve directly, but easy to verify”: the user may not know how to solve the problem or understand the reasoning process, but it is still possible for the user to provide minimal feedback on the final outcome (e.g., “this is not what I want”).
items.title: MINT: Evaluating LLMs in Multi-turn Interaction with Tools and Language Feedback

items.span_text: 2022); (2) then either interact with tools by generating Python code and executing it through a Python interpreter (“Execute:” in Fig.
items.title: MINT: Evaluating LLMs in Multi-turn Interaction with Tools and Language Feedback

items.span_text: 1), or proposing a solution to the user (“Propose Solution:” in Fig.
items.title: MINT: Evaluating LLMs in Multi-turn Interaction with Tools and Language Feedback

items.span_text: For each turn, the LLM can choose to interact with tools (generate code to call equation-solver in Fig.
items.title: MINT: Evaluating LLMs in Multi-turn Interaction with Tools and Language Feedback

items.span_text: To keep the LLM from getting stuck in an infinite loop of tool-calling without proposing a solution, MINT reminds the LLM: “You have X steps left and Y chances to propose solution left,” and provides an additional instruction at the last turn: “You should take the last step to propose a solution.” Intuitively, with more interaction with tools, the LLM can get more useful observations through the Python interpreter (e.g., calculation results, error messages).
items.title: MINT: Evaluating LLMs in Multi-turn Interaction with Tools and Language Feedback

items.span_text: Through prompting (§F.5.2), we encourage LLMs to use the Python interpreter to test their generated code on the provided public test suite for code generation problems before committing to a solution.
items.title: MINT: Evaluating LLMs in Multi-turn Interaction with Tools and Language Feedback

items.span_text: However, all these works only contain a single-step user interaction, where the human interacting with the agent provides an initial instruction containing all the required information.
items.title: tau-bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains

items.span_text: Task-oriented dialogue has been a long-standing challenge for NLP, with several efforts over the years to build domain-specific offline datasets or user simulators.
items.title: tau-bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains

items.span_text: The latter either rely on user simulators that are rule-based or rely on symbolic specifications [17, 8] or perform tests with real humans through crowdsourcing platforms [9].
items.title: tau-bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains

items.span_text: Some very recent work explores the use of LMs as response raters to train dialogue systems [10] or evaluates their capability for simulating users [7]. τ -bench leverages the powerful text generation capabilities of state-of-the-art LMs to simulate realistic user utterances and long-context conversations using textual scenario descriptions, with the goal of evaluating agents.
items.title: tau-bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains

items.span_text: The stochastic sampling from LMs allows for diverse yet faithful variations in the dialogue when re-run with the exact scenario – this is extremely useful for testing agent consistency, as we show in § 5.1.
items.title: tau-bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains

items.span_text: This will serve as a baseline for evaluating LLM’s ability to solve tool-augmented tasks and leverage natural language feedback.
items.title: MINT: Evaluating LLMs in Multi-turn Interaction with Tools and Language Feedback

items.span_text: We consider this as the case of Lazy User-LLM Interaction since the simulated user provides at most one additional binary feedback during interaction.
items.title: MINT: Evaluating LLMs in Multi-turn Interaction with Tools and Language Feedback

items.span_text: Informative user-LLM interaction with language feedback.
items.title: MINT: Evaluating LLMs in Multi-turn Interaction with Tools and Language Feedback

items.span_text: To address these issues, we prompt GPT-4 (§F.4.2) to simulate user language feedback (dotted boxes in Fig.
items.title: MINT: Evaluating LLMs in Multi-turn Interaction with Tools and Language Feedback

items.span_text: The agent interacts with both (1) databases (db) via API tools, and (2) a (simulated) user (user) to complete a task, i.e., S = Sdb ⊗ Suser , A = Adb ∪ Auser , O = Odb ∪ Ouser .
items.title: tau-bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains

items.span_text: In addition, the agent is provided a domain-specific policy document containing rules it must adhere to – one can think of this as partially describing the world model of the domain.
items.title: tau-bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains

items.span_text: The contents of the database form the state sdb (Figure 2a), which is hidden from the agent and the user, and can only be read from or written to using API actions adb , which are usually in the form tool_name(**kwargs).
items.title: tau-bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains

items.span_text: When an action is executed on the database, the transition Tdb : (sdb , adb ) 7→ (s′db , odb ) is deterministic and implemented as a Python function (Figure 2b).
items.title: tau-bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains

items.span_text: Each domain has a policy (Figure 2c) that explains the domain databases, task procedures, and restrictions for the agent to follow in its interactions.
items.title: tau-bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains

items.span_text: Some restrictions are implemented as checks in the API, e.g., using a payment ID not in the user profile will lead to odb = "Error: payment not found", and others not, e.g., the airline policy states different baggage allowances for different membership statuses and cabin classes, but the agent needs to fill in the number of baggage items to be paid for in the book_reservation API, similar to the freedom given real-world agents.
items.title: tau-bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains

items.span_text: The transition Tuser : (suser , auser ) 7→ (s′user , ouser ) is stochastic and attaches the agent’s message to the chat history followed by sampling a new user message from the LM, e.g., ouser can then be "Yes, I also want to cancel another flight." When the user issues ouser ="###STOP###", the episode finishes and the agent is evaluated.
items.title: tau-bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains

items.span_text: The instruction sets up user identity, intent, and preferences in a way that guarantees only one possible outcome under the domain policy.
items.title: tau-bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains

items.span_text: Each task episode consists of the simulated user starting with a request, which the agent handles in a conversational manner while being able to call tools at any point and refer to the provided policy.
items.title: tau-bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains

items.span_text: The reward of a task episode r = raction × routput ∈ {0, 1} is based on (1) whether the final database is identical to the unique ground truth outcome database (raction ), and (2) whether the agent’s responses to the user contain all necessary information (routput ).
items.title: tau-bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains

items.span_text: So for the task of Figure 2d, the agentuser dialogue can be varied and the agent can call various (read) actions, but the agent is successful if the only database write action is return_delivered_order_items(order_id="#W2890441", item_ids=["2366567022"], payment_method_id="credit_card_1061405"), and the user responses contain "54.04", "41.64" as substrings.
items.title: tau-bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains
